title: State Lottery Tax Rates: Every US Rate Explained
description: From 0% to 13.3%, state lottery tax rates vary dramatically. Discover exactly how much a jackpot winner keeps after federal and state taxes — with real dollar examples.
content:
Why State Lottery Taxes Can Swallow Half Your Jackpot

Imagine matching every number on a Powerball ticket — the five white balls and the red Powerball — and finding yourself suddenly worth hundreds of millions of dollars. Now imagine that before you spend a single cent, a government entity you may have never thought much about quietly takes more than half of it. That is not a hypothetical scare story. That is the mathematically verifiable reality for jackpot winners in certain US states, and it happens every time a major prize is claimed.

Most lottery players think about the jackpot number advertised on billboards and television tickers. Very few think carefully about state lottery tax rates — the layer of taxation that sits on top of federal withholding and can swing a winner's take-home total by tens of millions of dollars depending purely on their zip code. A winner in Florida and a winner in New York City who claim identical prizes on identical terms will walk away with dramatically different amounts of money. Understanding exactly why is not just academically interesting — it is the kind of financial literacy that separates a well-prepared windfall from a chaotic one.

This guide breaks down every layer of lottery taxation in the United States, from the mechanics of federal withholding to a full state-by-state comparison, with real dollar figures anchored to actual jackpot scenarios. The numbers here are drawn from current tax law as of March 2026.

How Lottery Taxes Work — Federal Withholding vs. State Withholding Explained

Lottery taxation operates in two distinct phases, and confusing them is one of the most common mistakes winners and lottery enthusiasts make when estimating a prize's true value.

Phase One: Federal Withholding at the Source

The IRS requires lottery operators to withhold 24% of any prize exceeding $5,000 before the winner receives a single dollar. This is an automatic, non-negotiable deduction applied at the point of payment. On a $10 million prize, the lottery agency sends $2.4 million directly to the federal government before the check is cut. The winner sees $7.6 million arrive in their account and sometimes makes the dangerous assumption that their federal tax obligation is settled.

It is not. The 24% withholding rate is a minimum deposit toward a tax bill, not the final bill itself. Because lottery winnings are classified as ordinary income, they push a winner's total taxable income into the highest federal tax bracket. In 2026, that top marginal rate is 37%. The arithmetic is unforgiving: a 24% withholding on prize income that will ultimately be taxed at 37% means winners owe an additional 13 percentage points when they file their annual return — a surprise bill that has blindsided more than a few unprepared jackpot claimants.

Phase Two: State Withholding

Layered on top of federal taxes is whatever the winner's home state — and in some cases, home city — chooses to collect. This is where the variation becomes dramatic. State lottery withholding rates range from a true zero in eight states to over 10% in a handful of high-tax jurisdictions. Unlike federal rates, which apply uniformly across the country, state rates are set by individual legislatures and vary based on a state's broader income tax structure and, occasionally, specific lottery tax statutes.

It is important to note that the relevant state is generally the winner's state of residence, not where the ticket was purchased — though some states tax non-residents on prizes won within their borders, creating a potential double-withholding scenario for cross-border ticket buyers. Always consult a tax professional in your specific situation.

State-by-State Lottery Tax Rates — Full Comparison Table with Dollar Impact

The table below reflects state income tax rates applied to lottery winnings as of early 2026. The "Dollar Impact" column shows how much a state's tax alone would cost on a hypothetical $100 million lump-sum prize after the lottery operator's cash-value reduction but before federal taxes. Use our tax calculator to run personalized scenarios with any prize amount.

Iowa8.53%$8.53MTop marginal rate applies
Wisconsin7.65%$7.65MTop marginal rate applies
Illinois4.95%$4.95MFlat state income tax rate
Michigan4.25%$4.25MFlat state income tax rate

Pennsylvania3.07%$3.07MFlat rate; one of the lowest taxing states

Note: Tax rates reflect 2026 law and are subject to legislative change. Local municipality taxes (such as New York City's) are separate from state rates and can significantly increase the total burden. Consult a licensed tax advisor for jurisdiction-specific guidance.

States With No Lottery Tax — The Zero-Rate States and What It Really Means

Eight states effectively impose zero state tax on lottery winnings: California, Florida, Texas, Washington, South Dakota, Wyoming, Tennessee, and New Hampshire. The reasons vary by state, which matters more than the outcome might suggest.

Florida, Texas, Washington, South Dakota, Wyoming, and Nevada have no broad-based state income tax at all — lottery winnings escape state taxation simply because the state doesn't tax income in general. California is a notable exception: it does have one of the nation's highest state income tax rates (up to 13.3% on income over $1 million), yet its state constitution specifically exempts lottery winnings from state income tax. New Hampshire similarly exempts lottery prizes by statute, even though it taxes certain investment income.

The counterintuitive reality: California, a state famous for its high income taxes, is one of the most tax-friendly jurisdictions in the entire country for lottery jackpot winners — solely because its constitution carves out an explicit exemption for lottery prizes. A California resident winning a $500 million jackpot saves over $30 million compared to a winner in neighboring Oregon, simply due to state-level policy differences.

However, "zero state tax" does not mean zero tax burden. Every winner in every state still faces federal income tax. The 24% withholding is applied regardless of the winner's home state, and the additional 13% owed at filing time (to reach the 37% top bracket) applies universally. Zero-rate states save winners from an additional layer of taxation — they do not eliminate the federal obligation.

Winners should also be aware that moving to a zero-tax state after winning but before claiming a prize is a tactic that has attracted IRS scrutiny. Tax authorities typically assess state liability based on residency at the time of the win, not at the time of the claim. Relocation strategies require careful legal and tax counsel. Explore our state lottery guide for more detail on how individual states handle multi-state game prizes.

Lump Sum vs. Annuity — How Your Payout Choice Multiplies the Tax Hit

Every major multi-state jackpot — including both Powerball and Mega Millions — offers winners a choice between two fundamentally different payment structures, and that choice has enormous tax implications that most casual players never fully consider.

The Lump Sum (Cash Value) Option

The lump sum, officially called the cash value option, delivers the entire prize as a single payment. The critical caveat: the cash value is not the advertised jackpot. It is typically 50–60% of the headline number, reflecting the present value of the annuity's future payments. A $500 million advertised jackpot typically carries a cash value around $239 million. That entire $239 million is then treated as ordinary income in a single tax year — pushing every dollar into the 37% federal bracket and triggering the full state tax rate simultaneously. The tax hit is concentrated, immediate, and substantial.

The Annuity Option

The annuity spreads payments over 29 annual installments (for both Powerball and Mega Millions), with each payment increasing by approximately 5% per year. The total payout over 29 years equals the full advertised jackpot. The tax advantage is meaningful: each annual installment, while still large, is a fraction of the lump sum — and each is taxed in its own tax year. This does not reduce the applicable tax rates significantly on major jackpots (the payments are still large enough to sit firmly in the top bracket), but it does spread the tax obligation over nearly three decades and eliminates the massive single-year tax event.

The annuity's true advantage is less about rate arbitrage and more about behavioral finance: receiving managed annual payments prevents the single-year tax avalanche and imposes a structure that many financial advisors argue protects winners from rapid wealth depletion. The lump sum's advantage is control — the winner can invest the after-tax sum and potentially outperform the annuity's guaranteed 5% annual growth, depending on market conditions over the payment period.

Real-World Dollar Examples — What a Powerball or Mega Millions Winner Actually Takes Home

Theory becomes viscerally clear with concrete numbers. Consider three hypothetical winners, each claiming a $500 million advertised Powerball jackpot via the lump-sum cash option of approximately $239 million.

Winner A: New York City Resident

This winner faces the steepest tax environment in the United States. The combined federal and state/city withholding alone is staggering:

  • Federal withholding (24%): $57.36 million withheld immediately
  • Additional federal tax owed at filing (13% to reach 37% top rate): approximately $31.07 million
  • New York State income tax (10.9%): approximately $26.05 million
  • New York City income tax (3.876%): approximately $9.26 million
  • Total taxes: approximately $123.74 million
  • Estimated take-home: approximately $115.26 million

On a $500 million headline jackpot, a New York City resident takes home roughly $115 million — less than 23 cents on every advertised dollar. The combined effective rate on the cash value exceeds 51%.

Winner B: Florida Resident

Florida imposes no state income tax, eliminating one entire layer of taxation:

  • Federal withholding (24%): $57.36 million
  • Additional federal tax at filing (13%): approximately $31.07 million
  • Florida state tax: $0
  • Total taxes: approximately $88.43 million
  • Estimated take-home: approximately $150.57 million

The Florida winner keeps roughly $35 million more than an identical winner in New York City, purely due to the difference in state and city tax policy — a gap that represents more than most Americans will earn in their entire working lives.

Winner C: New Jersey Resident

New Jersey's 10.75% rate on large prizes places it just below New York State in tax burden:

  • Federal withholding (24%): $57.36 million
  • Additional federal tax at filing (13%): approximately $31.07 million
  • New Jersey state tax (10.75%): approximately $25.69 million
  • Total taxes: approximately $114.12 million
  • Estimated take-home: approximately $124.88 million

These are estimates based on current rate structures and do not account for potential deductions, trust structures, or other tax-planning vehicles that a winner's advisors might employ. They illustrate, however, why tax planning is arguably the single most financially consequential decision a jackpot winner can make — dwarfing investment choices, real estate decisions, or charitable giving in its immediate dollar impact.

For deeper data on draw histories, number frequencies, and jackpot trends across both major multi-state games, explore our Powerball statistics and Mega Millions statistics pages, which draw on databases spanning over 1,900 and 2,400 draws respectively.

Understanding state lottery tax rates is not about discouraging participation in lottery games — it is about ensuring that anyone fortunate enough to win a significant prize approaches that moment with clear eyes and accurate expectations. The gap between an advertised jackpot and a deposited check is one of the largest, and least discussed, financial realities in American consumer life.

Lottery drawings are determined by random chance; all content on this page is for educational and entertainment purposes only. Nothing here constitutes tax or financial advice — consult a licensed tax professional for guidance specific to your situation.
